graph.topic: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
graph.researcher_bg: An HCI researcher specializing in Educational Technology (EdTech) and Human-AI Collaboration, focused on optimizing complex grading workflows and feedback quality.
graph.expert_bg: A seasoned university instructor with extensive experience in evaluating lengthy project reports

q_01__shared_root.label: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
q_01__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

q_01__exp_L1_00.label: Expert Grading Workflow
q_01__exp_L1_00.description: The end-to-end process of evaluating project reports, balancing efficiency with deep judgment.

q_01__exp_L2_00_00.label: Navigation & Pacing
q_01__exp_L2_00_00.description: Managing how I move through the document and the stack of assignments.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: The Holistic Skim
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: I always start by skimming the whole report to get the scope and direction before I dive into the details.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Non-Linear Navigation
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: I don't read straight through; I jump back and forth if the conclusion makes me question the methodology.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Batching Strategy
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: I debate whether to grade all Q1s at once for consistency or read full papers to understand the narrative.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_03.label: Fatigue & Autopilot
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_03.description: After about 10 reports, I catch myself grading format over content or just going through the motions.

q_01__exp_L2_00_01.label: Judgment & Calibration
q_01__exp_L2_00_01.description: Ensuring my scoring remains fair and stable across different students and ambiguous cases.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Rubric Friction
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: I get stuck when a student's work doesn't fit neatly into the rubric categories and have to second-guess my judgment.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Standard Drift
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: I keep a past 'A' example open to remind myself what excellence looks like so I don't drift too harsh or lenient.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Contrast Bias
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: If I just graded a terrible report, the next average one looks amazing, and I have to fight that contrast effect.

q_01__exp_L2_00_02.label: Diagnostic Intuition
q_01__exp_L2_00_02.description: Quick heuristic checks I perform to gauge effort and authenticity early on.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Effort Proxies
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: I check references and formatting first; if they're shaky, I assume the analysis underneath is rushed too.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Authenticity Checks
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: I can feel it when the writing style suddenly changes from casual to overly academic; usually means something was copied.

q_01__exp_L2_00_03.label: Feedback Construction
q_01__exp_L2_00_03.description: The cognitive work of translating my evaluation into actionable guidance for the student.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_03_00.label: Comment Synthesis
q_01__exp_leaf_00_03_00.description: The real work is organizing my scattered notes into feedback that guides them without being too harsh.

q_01__shared_root.label: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
q_01__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

q_01__res_L1_00.label: Digital Toolchain & Workspace Configuration
q_01__res_L1_00.description: Examining the software ecosystem and physical setup used to execute the grading process.

q_01__res_L2_00_00.label: Software Interoperability
q_01__res_L2_00_00.description: Understanding how different digital tools are linked or manually bridged during grading.

q_01__res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Map application switching sequences
q_01__res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Identify the specific order of applications used (e.g., LMS -> PDF Editor -> Spreadsheet) and where manual data transfer occurs.

q_01__res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Assess multi-window dependency
q_01__res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Determine if educators rely on multiple monitors or window splitting to view rubrics and submissions simultaneously.

q_01__res_L2_00_01.label: Modality Switching
q_01__res_L2_00_01.description: Investigating if the workflow transitions between digital and physical mediums.

q_01__res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Detect physical-digital hybridization
q_01__res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Determine if any part of the workflow involves printing physical copies for annotation before digitizing the final grade.

q_01__res_L1_01.label: Information Foraging & Navigation Patterns
q_01__res_L1_01.description: Analyzing how educators move through student content and manage task transitions.

q_01__res_L2_01_00.label: Document Traversal
q_01__res_L2_01_00.description: The specific path taken through the content of a single assignment.

q_01__res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Trace information foraging path
q_01__res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Map the linear or non-linear order in which sections of a report are read (e.g., Abstract first vs. Code first vs. Rubric first).

q_01__res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Identify batching granularity
q_01__res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Understand the unit of switching between students (e.g., grading all Introductions across class vs. grading Student A completely).

q_01__res_L2_01_01.label: Rubric Integration Point
q_01__res_L2_01_01.description: The temporal relationship between viewing criteria and viewing student work.

q_01__res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Pinpoint rubric consultation timing
q_01__res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Clarify exactly when the grading rubric is opened relative to reading the content (pre-reading, concurrent, or post-reading).

q_01__res_L1_02.label: Feedback Artifact Construction
q_01__res_L1_02.description: Focusing on the mechanics of creating and finalizing the feedback output.

q_01__res_L2_02_00.label: Comment Generation Strategy
q_01__res_L2_02_00.description: How the textual feedback is composed and input into the system.

q_01__res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Quantify reusable component usage
q_01__res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Investigate the ratio of pre-written comment banks versus unique typed comments during the workflow.

q_01__res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Map feedback spatial anchoring
q_01__res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Identify where feedback is anchored within the interface (in-margin comments vs. summary box vs. separate document).

q_01__res_L2_02_01.label: Finalization Protocol
q_01__res_L2_02_01.description: The specific steps taken to complete the grading transaction.

q_01__res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Describe verification mechanisms
q_01__res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Identify if there is a specific review step where grades are summed or adjusted before final submission to the LMS.

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Walking around a house before entering

structural_mapping.inputs: Full report document vs. Entire house exterior
structural_mapping.logic: Skim for scope and direction vs. Walk perimeter for stability
structural_mapping.outputs: Context for grading vs. Decision to enter

analogy.explanation: The researcher wants a log of which rooms you entered first (Trace), but you are walking the perimeter to check the foundation (Skim); tracking entry order misses the goal of assessing overall viability before committing.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you start grading, do you remember which section you read first, or do you just flip through the whole thing to get the gist before settling in?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Glancing at a map before a trip

structural_mapping.inputs: Report content and rubric criteria
structural_mapping.logic: Single orientation pass to gauge scope
structural_mapping.outputs: Sense of direction before detailed work

analogy.explanation: The researcher seeks distinct timing for rubric checks, but the expert's skim functions like glancing at a map: you absorb the route and legend together in one look, making separate timing hard to isolate.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you do that initial holistic skim, are you mentally checking rubric criteria simultaneously, or is that purely for gist before you officially open the rubric?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Following a hunch

structural_mapping.inputs: A specific discrepancy or suspicion (e.g., conclusion doesn't match data)
structural_mapping.logic: Conditional jump to the source of evidence to verify the suspicion
structural_mapping.outputs: Confirmed understanding or identified flaw (rather than just completing a section)

analogy.explanation: The researcher's concept tracks the physical steps taken, similar to how 'following a hunch' involves moving, but the target concept clarifies that the expert's movement is conditional on a trigger. Like a detective chasing a clue rather than a tourist following a map, the expert jumps based on content quality, not a predetermined sequence.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you jump back to the methods because the conclusion feels off, is that a planned step in your route, or just you chasing a suspicion in the moment?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Sorting laundry by color vs. washing one person's clothes

structural_mapping.inputs: Pile of reports (Stack of dirty clothes)
structural_mapping.logic: Group by question type (Sort by color) vs. Group by student (Sort by owner)
structural_mapping.outputs: Completed grades (Clean clothes)

analogy.explanation: The researcher tracks your path through one person's load (single student report), but you might wash all whites from everyone first (batching questions), which changes how attention flows across the whole stack.

assistance_payload.scenario: When grading, do you finish one student's whole paper before touching the next, or do you grade question one for everyone first?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Driving home on autopilot

structural_mapping.inputs: Starting a repetitive task (grading reports vs. driving a familiar route)
structural_mapping.logic: Repetition triggers habitual execution over active attention
structural_mapping.outputs: Task completion with reduced detail awareness (format over content vs. missing scenery)

analogy.explanation: The researcher's reading order map records which sections are visited, but like driving home on autopilot, the expert may follow the same route without actively processing the scenery due to fatigue.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you reach that tenth report, do you still read each section deeply, or do you find yourself visiting the same spots without really processing the content?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Checking a recipe but cooking from memory

structural_mapping.inputs: Intention to follow guidelines (Rubric/Recipe)
structural_mapping.logic: Fatigue causes bypass of detailed criteria (Autopilot)
structural_mapping.outputs: Superficial compliance instead of deep adherence (Format/Habit)

analogy.explanation: The researcher assumes opening the rubric ensures criteria usage, like opening a recipe ensures following steps. But like skimming a report when tired, you might open the recipe but cook from memory anyway; the tool is accessed, but fatigue triggers autopilot, invalidating the timing metric.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you're ten reports in and tired, do you ever open the rubric but find yourself ignoring the specific criteria and just grading on instinct?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes that high reliance on standard responses often signals cognitive disengagement and grading fatigue.

pivot.limitation: If the discussion remains only on the feeling of autopilot, the researcher misses the opportunity to measure the specific behavioral patterns driving that state.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher seeks to quantify the usage of reusable components to establish a baseline for feedback construction efficiency.
pivot.compelling_reason: While the expert's warning about quality is vital, the researcher needs this metric to distinguish between helpful efficiency and harmful disengagement within the system design.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I understand that using pre-written comments can feel like going through the motions, but measuring exactly how often you rely on them versus typing new thoughts helps us determine if the system is supporting you or contributing to that fatigue.

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Noticing you're driving on autopilot

structural_mapping.inputs: Repetitive task progression (10th report / Long highway stretch)
structural_mapping.logic: Awareness of focus drift triggers correction (Catching myself / Checking speed)
structural_mapping.outputs: Restored accuracy (Grading content again / Staying in lane)

analogy.explanation: The researcher's 'verification mechanism' behaves like the target concept of noticing driving drift: it is not a scheduled stop, but an internal alarm that triggers correction the moment focus slips, matching the expert's 'catching myself' rather than a formal review step.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you catch yourself drifting into autopilot, do you consider that moment of self-correction to be your verification step, even if you don't write it down?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Stopping to check the recipe

structural_mapping.inputs: Student work and rubric criteria
structural_mapping.logic: Mismatch detected triggers uncertainty and a search for validation
structural_mapping.outputs: Delayed judgment or tool switch to find clarity

analogy.explanation: The researcher records the 'app switch' as a regular step, but like a cook checking the recipe only when the dish tastes wrong, the expert switches tools only when judgment feels uncertain, marking a cognitive hurdle rather than a routine action.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you switch between the PDF and the rubric sheet, is that a routine step, or does it happen specifically because the student's work doesn't fit the boxes?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert is emphasizing the cognitive load encountered when student work does not align neatly with rubric categories, causing hesitation in judgment.

pivot.limitation: Staying only on this friction risks missing how the expert structurally adapts their workflow to manage these high-load moments.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand the batching granularity, specifically whether the expert grades by student or by section across the class.
pivot.compelling_reason: Connecting the friction to the batching strategy reveals whether the expert's workflow is static or dynamically adjusted to handle cognitive bottlenecks.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I know navigating those rubric mismatches is where the real work happens, but understanding whether you switch students or sections during those stuck moments helps us see how you manage that load.

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Stopping at a confusing instruction

structural_mapping.inputs: Student work and rubric categories
structural_mapping.logic: Attempting to match evidence to fixed criteria
structural_mapping.outputs: Judgment hesitation and second-guessing

analogy.explanation: Just as stopping at a confusing instruction reveals a map error, the moment you open the rubric (timing) is where the fit error (friction) becomes visible, linking the timestamp to the judgment struggle.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you open the rubric while reading, do you ever freeze because the student's work doesn't fit the boxes, forcing you to second-guess the tool instead of just assigning a score?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the internal cognitive friction and second-guessing that occurs when student work does not align neatly with rubric categories.

pivot.limitation: Focusing exclusively on this psychological experience may obscure the concrete procedural safeguards established to manage that uncertainty.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher seeks to identify specific verification mechanisms, such as grade summation or adjustment protocols, that occur before final submission.
pivot.compelling_reason: Understanding these procedural steps is essential to determine how institutional workflows support or constrain expert intuition during moments of rubric friction.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I hear that the uncertainty is really challenging for you personally, and I want to understand how that uncertainty translates into the specific checks or review steps you take before locking in the grade.

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Keeping a paint sample chip visible while matching colors

structural_mapping.inputs: Current student work and a past 'A' example
structural_mapping.logic: Visual comparison to prevent internal standard shift
structural_mapping.outputs: Consistent grade assignment

analogy.explanation: The researcher assumes the second window displays rules (rubric), but like a painter keeping a sample chip visible to prevent color drift, you use it for concrete calibration to maintain grading consistency over time.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you split your screen to grade, is that second window mostly for checking rubric boxes, or do you keep a past 'A' paper open to calibrate your eye?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Comparing paint to a sample chip

structural_mapping.inputs: New wall section (Student work), Old paint chip (Past 'A' example), Color name label (Rubric)
structural_mapping.logic: Visual side-by-side matching (Exemplar calibration) vs. Reading text specifications (Rubric consultation)
structural_mapping.outputs: Confidence in color match (Grade consistency)

analogy.explanation: Just as asking when a painter reads the color name misses that they are matching a chip for accuracy, asking about rubric timing misses that you use a past 'A' for visual calibration; both aim for consistency, but the tool shifts from text to example.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you keep a past 'A' example open to prevent drifting, do you find the rubric timing matters less because the example becomes your primary standard instead?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Keeping your balance while walking

structural_mapping.inputs: Uneven ground (varying student work quality)
structural_mapping.logic: Constant micro-adjustments (referencing past 'A' example)
structural_mapping.outputs: Staying upright (consistent grading standards)

analogy.explanation: The researcher expects a final safety check (looking back after walking), but like Contrast Bias, the expert's verification is continuous (keeping balance), so corrections happen during the act rather than in a final review step.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you keep that past 'A' example open, does that help you set the grade correctly the first time, or do you still need to review all grades again before submitting?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes a critical cognitive risk where grading sequence influences judgment accuracy through contrast effects. This psychological factor is vital for understanding grading reliability.

pivot.limitation: If the conversation remains solely on the expert's cognitive bias, the structural workflow decisions that trigger that bias remain undefined for the researcher.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand the logistical unit of work, specifically whether the expert batches by student or by question across students.
pivot.compelling_reason: Understanding the batching granularity is necessary for the researcher to map where the expert's contrast bias is most likely to occur within the workflow.
pivot.coarse_scenario: That contrast effect is really important, and to see where it hits hardest, I need to understand if you grade all of Student A's work before moving to Student B, or if you grade all Introductions across the class at once.

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Steering a car in strong wind

structural_mapping.inputs: Previous grade impact and current student work
structural_mapping.logic: Continuous mental adjustments to neutralize bias
structural_mapping.outputs: Fair grade assignment

analogy.explanation: The researcher expects verification to be a single destination check (like looking at a map once), but the expert's process behaves like steering in wind: accuracy comes from constant, invisible corrections rather than a single discrete step.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you finish a stack, do you have a specific step to review grades, or is that checking happening continuously in your head while you read each one?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Tapping a watermelon to check ripeness

structural_mapping.inputs: Surface cues (formatting/style vs sound/skin texture)
structural_mapping.logic: Heuristic inference (shaky surface implies poor interior)
structural_mapping.outputs: Early decision on quality validity (triage vs purchase)

analogy.explanation: Both concepts act as quality filters. Like tapping a watermelon, the expert checks the surface first to predict the inside before investing time, whereas the researcher assumes the check happens at the end to count the total.

assistance_payload.scenario: Before you even read the analysis, do you scan the references to gauge effort, or is your main verification step just summing the points at the very end?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Smelling milk to check if it is sour

structural_mapping.inputs: Surface details like formatting and references
structural_mapping.logic: Surface flaws indicate deeper quality issues
structural_mapping.outputs: Decision to grade strictly or skip deep reading

analogy.explanation: The researcher tracks where you look (foraging path), which is the physical trace of your quality check, similar to mapping the steps of someone smelling milk; the movement exists, but the goal is the sniff.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you feel that sudden shift in writing style, do you jump to specific sections to confirm it, meaning your reading order follows the suspicion rather than the document structure?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Sniff test for spoilage

structural_mapping.inputs: Sensory cue (smell or formatting/style)
structural_mapping.logic: Deviation from norm indicates underlying failure
structural_mapping.outputs: Reject or Accept content

analogy.explanation: Both concepts involve a verification step before finalizing, but the expert's acts like a sniff test (qualitative integrity) similar to effort proxies, whereas the researcher's assumes a count (quantitative total). The target concept clarifies that the expert is checking content safety, not arithmetic accuracy.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you mention verifying the work before submission, do you mean checking the math adds up, or do you mean sensing if the student actually wrote it?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

analogy.target_concept: Adjusting a recipe to taste

structural_mapping.inputs: Bank comments or raw notes
structural_mapping.logic: Tone calibration and organization
structural_mapping.outputs: Guiding feedback

analogy.explanation: The researcher measures effort by ingredient origin (bank vs. typed), but like a chef, the expert knows the workload lies in adjusting the seasoning (tone) to fit the diner (student), regardless of who chopped the vegetables.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you reuse a past comment, do you still spend significant mental energy adapting it so it doesn't sound too harsh for that specific student?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes that organizing scattered notes into constructive feedback is the core challenge of the grading workflow. This cognitive effort is central to their experience.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on the cognitive organization misses how the mechanical act of switching applications might be fragmenting that thought process.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand the specific sequence of software interactions to identify where manual data transfer occurs.
pivot.compelling_reason: While the cognitive load is primary, the toolchain structure often dictates where that load is highest, making the mechanical mapping essential for holistic improvement.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I agree that synthesizing the comments is the real work, but if we map out exactly which tools you switch between to do that, we might find where the software is adding unnecessary friction to that thinking process.

q_02__shared_root.label: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
q_02__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

q_02__exp_L1_00.label: Expert Instructor Perspective
q_02__exp_L1_00.description: Seasoned university instructor evaluating lengthy project reports, focusing on cognitive load, consistency, and intuitive judgments.

q_02__exp_L2_00_00.label: Cognitive Load & Consistency
q_02__exp_L2_00_00.description: The mental effort required to maintain fair standards across a large batch of submissions while managing fatigue and variability.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Standard Drift & Fatigue
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: You start strict on the first few, but by the tenth, fatigue or weird quality swings make you let slides slip. You realize you're grading against a moving target.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Grading Confidence Erosion
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: When work varies so much, you start second-guessing if you're applying the same standard. Adds a lot of mental weight and slows you down.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Rubric Dimension Juggling
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: Trying to track research, structure, and clarity all at once. Easy to get lost in the details and forget to check the big picture.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_03.label: Context Switching Tax
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_03.description: Every time I switch from Student A to B, I lose five minutes recalibrating to their topic. It adds up fast.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_04.label: Intuition Transparency Tension
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_04.description: By the end, you trust your gut to finish on time, but you worry it's not transparent or defensible if a student asks.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_05.label: The 'Word Salad' Radar
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_05.description: That gut feeling when a student uses fifty words to say something that should take ten. You know they're padding.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_06.label: The 'Too Polished' Uncanny Valley
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_06.description: Writing is so perfect it feels sterile, like a machine wrote it. Plagiarism checker says clean, but your gut says 'uncanny valley'.

q_02__exp_L2_00_01.label: Workflow & Logistics
q_02__exp_L2_00_01.description: The practical constraints of time and volume that dictate how deeply I can engage with each report.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Page Count Overwhelm
q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: 40 students x 30 pages = 1,200 pages. Just the scrolling becomes a bottleneck before you read a word.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: The Feedback Time Box
q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: I only have 20 minutes per report. I have to decide: fix their grammar or focus on the core argument? Often lose the holistic view.

q_02__exp_L2_00_02.label: Assessment Integrity

q_03__exp_L1_00.label: The Grading Marathon Mindset
q_03__exp_L1_00.description: The psychological framing and emotional preparation required before engaging with a large stack of end-of-term projects.

q_03__exp_L2_00_00.label: Emotional Framing
q_03__exp_L2_00_00.description: How I mentally brace myself for the volume and weight of the work ahead.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: The "Mountain View" Dread
q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: It's that moment Sunday night when you see the submission count hit 45. You feel a grim determination but also physically feel the weight of the hours before you even open a file.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: The Mercy Budget
q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: I consciously decide how much emotional energy I can afford to spend on each student. If I care too much about one, I'll have nothing left for the last ten.

q_03__exp_L2_00_01.label: Strategic Detachment
q_03__exp_L2_00_01.description: Necessary psychological distancing to maintain efficiency without burning out.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Compartmentalizing Empathy
q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: You have to turn off the 'teacher care' mode slightly to survive the volume. It feels cold, but if I feel every student's struggle, I'll never finish.

q_03__exp_L1_01.label: The Exhaustion Peaks
q_03__exp_L1_01.description: Specific phases within the grading workflow where cognitive load and fatigue spike most severely.

q_03__exp_L2_01_00.label: The Middle Slump
q_03__exp_L2_01_00.description: The phase midway through the stack where novelty wears off and mental resistance peaks.

q_03__exp_leaf_01_00_00.label: The "Sameness" Blur
q_03__exp_leaf_01_00_00.description: Around report #15, everyone starts sounding like the same person. You stop seeing 'Sarah' or 'Mike' and just see 'The Report' due to repetition and same mistakes.

q_03__exp_leaf_01_00_01.label: Decision Fatigue Wall
q_03__exp_leaf_01_00_01.description: By the middle, tracking inconsistencies (like methodology vs conclusion) feels like juggling plates. Deciding if a paragraph is 'B+' or 'A-' feels like lifting weights.

q_03__exp_L2_01_01.label: The Feedback Crunch
q_03__exp_L2_01_01.description: The final stage where translating judgments into written comments becomes the primary bottleneck.

q_03__exp_leaf_01_01_00.label: The Translation Tax
q_03__exp_leaf_01_01_00.description: Reading is easy; writing the comment that doesn't crush their soul but fixes the error? That's where my brain fries. Often I'm just writing the same few comments over and over.

q_03__exp_leaf_01_01_01.label: The "Good Enough" Surrender
q_03__exp_leaf_01_01_01.description: You hit a point where you know this comment isn't perfect, or that you're grading by instinct, and guilt creeps in about doing justice to their work. But you hit send to sleep.

q_03__exp_L1_02.label: Tacit Survival Mechanisms
q_03__exp_L1_02.description: Unspoken heuristics and intuitive triggers used to manage energy and maintain sanity during the grading marathon.

q_03__exp_L2_02_00.label: Energy Conservation
q_03__exp_L2_02_00.description: Subconscious strategies to preserve mental stamina across the entire batch.

q_03__exp_leaf_02_00_00.label: The "Deep Dive" Rationing
q_03__exp_leaf_02_00_00.description: I unconsciously pick only one or two papers to read deeply. The rest get the standard scan. It's unfair, but it's how I survive the stack.

q_03__exp_leaf_02_00_01.label: The "Red Flag" Trigger
q_03__exp_leaf_02_00_01.description: When I see a specific formatting error early, my energy drops instantly because I know the rest is messy. It's a somatic warning sign.

structural_mapping.logic: Limited energy forces selecting only one or two for full attention while the rest get scanned
structural_mapping.outputs: Completing the task but feeling guilty about uneven attention vs. Clearing inbox but feeling guilty about missing details

analogy.explanation: Both situations involve emotional toll stemming from rationing attention due to overload, not the outcome itself. Just as you feel guilty for skimming a friend's long message rather than the reply you send, the expert feels toll from not reading deeply, not from assigning the low grade.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you're halfway through a heavy stack, does the emotional weight come from assigning low grades, or from knowing you couldn't read every paper as deeply as you wanted?

q_04__exp_L1_00.label: Substantive Quality Cues
q_04__exp_L1_00.description: The core intellectual signals that distinguish deep understanding from surface-level completion.

q_04__exp_L2_00_00.label: Argumentative Cohesion
q_04__exp_L2_00_00.description: How well the report's narrative holds together from start to finish.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: The "Promise-Payoff" Gap
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: I check if the conclusion actually answers the specific question posed in the introduction, or if they went off on a tangent and forgot their original goal.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Narrative Flow & Transitions
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: If the report jumps around without clear transitions, it's a red flag that the thinking isn't solid or the logic has gaps.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Conclusion Specificity
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: A C-grade conclusion is generic enough to fit any project; an A-grade conclusion references specific data points unique to their work.

q_04__exp_L2_00_01.label: Analytical Depth
q_04__exp_L2_00_01.description: Evidence that the student processed information rather than just reporting it.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: The "So What?" Factor
q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: I look for the moment where they stop describing what happened or restating the textbook, and start explaining why it matters or what it implies.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Limitation Acknowledgement
q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Strong students admit where their work is weak; weaker students pretend their methodology was flawless to hide uncertainty.

q_04__exp_L1_01.label: Presentation & Authenticity
q_04__exp_L1_01.description: How the physical layout, tone, and voice support or hinder the grading process and signal effort.

q_04__exp_L2_01_00.label: Visual & Formatting Signals
q_04__exp_L2_01_00.description: The relationship between figures, tables, and the written narrative, plus attention to detail.

q_04__exp_leaf_01_00_00.label: Figure Self-Sufficiency
q_04__exp_leaf_01_00_00.description: I shouldn't need to read the paragraph to understand the graph; the axis labels and trends should be obvious at a glance.

q_04__exp_leaf_01_00_01.label: Formatting as Effort Proxy
q_04__exp_leaf_01_00_01.description: Good reports use formatting to guide my eye; sloppy formatting or typos make me question if they even cared enough to proofread.

q_04__exp_L2_01_01.label: Voice & Source Integration
q_04__exp_L2_01_01.description: How external references are woven into the student's own voice and tone.

q_04__exp_leaf_01_01_00.label: Citation Contextualization
q_04__exp_leaf_01_01_00.description: I can tell instantly if they read the paper or just grabbed the abstract; A-grade work weaves sources into their own argument.

q_04__exp_leaf_01_01_01.label: Tone & Voice Consistency
q_04__exp_leaf_01_01_01.description: If the writing sounds like they're trying too hard to sound smart, or the tone shifts suddenly, it feels inauthentic or copied.

q_04__exp_L1_02.label: Pedagogical Translation
q_04__exp_L1_02.description: The challenge of converting expert instinct into teachable guidelines for novices.

q_04__exp_L2_02_00.label: Articulating Tacit Knowledge
q_04__exp_L2_02_00.description: The difficulty of putting vague feelings of quality into concrete words for a TA.

q_04__exp_leaf_02_00_00.label: Rubric Translation Difficulty
q_04__exp_leaf_02_00_00.description: It's hard to write a rubric line for 'good flow' without sounding vague, so TAs struggle to apply it consistently.

q_04__exp_leaf_02_00_01.label: Nuance Loss in Guidelines
q_04__exp_leaf_02_00_01.description: When I simplify my rules for a TA, they often miss the edge cases where a rule shouldn't apply.

q_04__exp_L2_02_01.label: Calibration Strategies
q_04__exp_L2_02_01.description: Methods used to align the TA's judgment with the instructor's standards.

q_04__exp_leaf_02_01_00.label: Example-Based Calibration
q_04__exp_leaf_02_01_00.description: Instead of explaining the rule, I just show them a past A and a past C and ask them to spot the differences themselves.

q_04__exp_leaf_02_01_01.label: Feedback Loop Timing
q_04__exp_leaf_02_01_01.description: If I don't check their first few grades immediately, they might drift off-standard before I catch it.

q_04__shared_root.label: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .
q_04__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Exploring challenges, cognitive load, and expert intuition in grading project-based assignments .

q_04__res_L1_00.label: Tacit Knowledge Extraction & Feature Specification
q_04__res_L1_00.description: Focuses on translating implicit expert judgments into explicit, observable data features that can be measured or flagged.

q_04__res_L2_00_00.label: Low-Level Artifact Signals
q_04__res_L2_00_00.description: Identifying concrete, surface-level markers in the document that correlate with expert quality assessments.

q_04__res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Textual & Visual Marker Identification
q_04__res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Determine which specific textual patterns (e.g., sentence complexity) or visual elements (e.g., figure resolution) serve as primary triggers for the expert's initial quality estimate.

q_04__res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Professionalism Quantification
q_04__res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Define objective metrics for 'polish' (e.g., citation consistency, whitespace usage) that experts use as proxies for student effort and care.

q_04__res_L2_00_01.label: High-Level Narrative Patterns
q_04__res_L2_00_01.description: Uncovering structural and logical cues that indicate depth of understanding beyond surface formatting.

q_04__res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Argument Flow Metrics
q_04__res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Explore how experts intuitively sense 'coherence' and whether this can be mapped to transition frequency or section balance ratios.

q_04__res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Evidence Quality Indicators
q_04__res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Isolate specific types of evidence (e.g., data density vs. anecdotal claims) that disproportionately influence the expert's grade prediction.

q_04__res_L1_01.label: Novice Scaffolding & Knowledge Transfer
q_04__res_L1_01.description: Examines how extracted cues can be structured to train novice TAs without overwhelming their cognitive capacity.

q_04__res_L2_01_00.label: Heuristic Operationalization
q_04__res_L2_01_00.description: Converting subjective expert language into actionable, binary, or countable checks for novices.

q_04__res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Subjective-to-Objective Translation
q_04__res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Investigate how to rewrite vague rubric terms (e.g., 'deep analysis') into the specific objective cues identified by the expert.

q_04__res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Triage Cue Identification
q_04__res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Identify 'dealbreaker' cues that allow novices to quickly flag low-quality work for deeper review, optimizing their grading time.

q_04__res_L2_01_01.label: Training Sequencing
q_04__res_L2_01_01.description: Determining the optimal order and method for introducing these cues to novice graders.

q_04__res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Cue Introduction Order
q_04__res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Understand whether novices should be trained on surface cues (formatting) first or structural cues (argument) to build accurate intuition fastest.

q_04__res_L1_02.label: Human-AI System Design Implications
q_04__res_L1_02.description: Explores how these objective cues inform the design of AI-assisted grading tools and their boundaries.

q_04__res_L2_02_00.label: Automation Boundaries
q_04__res_L2_02_00.description: Defining which cues are safe for AI to flag versus those that require human contextual judgment.

q_04__res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Safe-to-Auto Cue Selection
q_04__res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Determine which objective cues have low false-positive rates and can be automated without risking unfair penalization.

q_04__res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Uncertainty Thresholds
q_04__res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Establish confidence levels where the system should defer to human judgment despite the presence of positive or negative cues.

q_04__res_L2_02_01.label: Adversarial Robustness
q_04__res_L2_02_01.description: Assessing the risk of students manipulating objective cues to game the grading system.

q_04__res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Cue Gaming Risk Assessment
q_04__res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Evaluate the likelihood that making these cues explicit will lead students to optimize for the metric rather than the learning outcome.

pivot.limitation: Focusing exclusively on the expert's detailed verification overlooks the researcher's need to understand how novices manage cognitive load through faster initial assessments.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand how to operationalize heuristics that allow for quick triage of low-quality submissions.
pivot.compelling_reason: Balancing the expert's depth with the researcher's efficiency goals ensures the resulting scaffolding is both rigorous and practical for novice graders.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I understand that checking the promise-payoff gap is essential for final quality, but could you also share if there are any immediate dealbreakers in the intro that help you decide whether a paper is worth that deep read?

structural_mapping.outputs: Trust or grade based on content delivery vs. appearance

analogy.explanation: Both concepts act as quality checks, but while the expert's logic verifies the contents match the promise (substance), the researcher's metric checks if the receipt is printed neatly (presentation); both assess the transaction, but only one confirms the actual value received.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on distinguishing A from C grades overlooks the immediate need to help novices identify fundamentally flawed submissions that require urgent attention.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher aims to uncover heuristic cues that allow inexperienced graders to efficiently triage assignments before applying nuanced grading criteria.
pivot.compelling_reason: While precision in grading excellence is vital, establishing a baseline for filtering low-quality work ensures that grading time is allocated effectively across all submissions.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I see how specificity separates an A from a C, but could we also look for signs that would tell a new grader a project is fundamentally incomplete so they know to flag it immediately?

structural_mapping.logic: Testing physical alignment

assistance_payload.scenario: When you spot a generic conclusion, you're noticing missing context; do you worry a system might flag unique but valid work because it can't read that context?

analogy.target_concept: Listening for insight vs. news

structural_mapping.inputs: Student submission / Conversation content
structural_mapping.logic: Detecting shift from description to implication / Detecting shift from facts to meaning
structural_mapping.outputs: Quality judgment / Sense of value

analogy.explanation: The analogy shows that surface features (like voice clarity) don't guarantee deep meaning (like insight). The researcher measures the 'container' (text/visuals), but the expert evaluates the 'content shift' (description to implication), which surface markers cannot detect.

assistance_payload.scenario: Have you ever graded a visually perfect project where the figures were crisp, but you still gave it a low mark because the student never explained why their findings mattered?

structural_mapping.outputs: Coherence score vs. Quality judgment

analogy.explanation: Just as 'Limitation Acknowledgement' separates honest students from smooth liars, the 'So What?' factor separates deep work from empty work. The researcher's metrics measure the 'Sounding confident' layer, missing the 'Being right' layer you actually value.
